coredata.dc:title: Nitroreductase-responsive nanoparticles for in situ fluorescence imaging and synergistic antibacterial therapy of bacterial keratitis
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Jing
Zou, Ruifen
Wang, Pin
Wang, Xinfangzi
He, Xuefei
Liu, Fang
Xu, Chen
Wu, Aiguo
coredata.dc:description: As bacterial keratitis progresses rapidly, prompt intervention is necessary. Current diagnostic processes are time-consuming and invasive, leading to improper antibiotics for treatment. Therefore, innovative strategies for diagnosing and treating bacterial keratitis are urgently needed. In this study, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were developed by loading nitroreductase-responsive probes (NTRPs) onto Cu2-xSe@BSA. These nanoparticles exhibited integrated fluorescence imaging and antibacterial capabilities. In vitro and in vivo experiments showed that the nanoparticles produced responsive fluorescence signals in bacteria within 30 min due to an interaction between the released NTRP and bacterial endogenous nitroreductase (NTR). When combined with low-temperature photothermal therapy (PTT), the nanoparticles effectively eliminated E. coli and S. aureus, achieved antibacterial efficacy above 95% and facilitated the re-epithelialization process at the corneal wound site in vivo. Overall, the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles demonstrated potential for rapid, noninvasive in situ diagnosis, treatment, and visualization assessment of therapy effectiveness in bacterial keratitis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP. (a) TEM image of the Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles. (b) TEM image, (c) high-resolution TEM image, and (d) elemental mapping images of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP showing that the particle size, lattice structure and elemental distribution are consistent with those of Cu2-xSe@BSA. (e) The hydrodynamic particle size distributions of Cu2-xSe@BSA and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP. (f) The zeta potentials of Cu2-xSe@BSA and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP.

Responsiveness of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP to NTR. (a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited an absorption peak consistent with that of NTRP. (b) The absorption spectrum of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP showed an enhanced peak at approximately 400–500 nm and a decreased peak at 780 nm after reaction with NTR. (c) The emission spectra of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP before and after reaction with NTRP. (d) The absorption spectra of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP upon NTR treatment in PBS at different pH values. (e) NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP. (f) Semiquantitative fluorescence intensity of NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP in PBS at different pH values. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

Photothermal performance of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and copper ions release. (a) Photothermal heating curves of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP solutions at different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 80, 160 μg/mL) irradiated by an 808 nm NIR laser (1 W/cm2, 10 min). (b) Photothermal heating curves of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP solutions at a concentration of 40 μg/mL irradiated by an 808 nm laser at different laser power densities (0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5 W/cm2, 10 min). (c) Photothermal stability of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP solution over five cycles of heating/cooling, during which heating was performed by irradiation with an 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2). (d) Photothermal heating curves and linear fitting of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP (40 μg/mL). (e) Cumulative amounts of copper ions released from the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP suspension with or without exposure to the 808 nm laser (1 W/cm2). (f) Cumulative release amounts of copper ions from the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP suspension in PBS at different pH values.

Bacteria-responsive fluorescence imaging of bacteria in vitro. (a, b) Photographs of E. coli- and S. aureus-responsive fluorescence images at different times. As the reaction time was extended, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased and reached its highest value at 6 h. (c) Semiquantitative fluorescence intensity of E. coli-responsive suspensions. (d) Semiquantitative fluorescence intensity of S. aureus-responsive suspensions.

In vitro antibacterial performance of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP with/without laser irradiation. (a) Photothermal heating curves of bacteria incubated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 80, 120 μg/mL) and subsequently irradiated by an 808 nm laser (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min). (b, c) The optical density at 600 nm of E. coli and S. aureus suspensions that were administered different treatments. (d) Photographs of the bacterial culture plates of E. coli and S. aureus after different treatments. (e) Corresponding quantitative histogram of the bacterial survival rate determined by counting the number of colonies in the photographs. **p < 0.01.

Confocal images of live/dead staining assays for E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) via SYTO 9 (green, live bacteria) and PI (red, dead bacteria) staining. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Morphology of bacteria after different treatments. (a) SEM micrographs of E. coli and S. aureus subjected to different treatments. Under 808 nm laser irradiation, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP severely damaged the bacterial cell wall/membranes of both strains of bacteria, distorted their original morphology and generated clear lesions and holes in the cell wall/membranes. (b) TEM micrographs of E. coli and S. aureus after different treatments. The Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group caused the most severe structural damage to E. coli and S. aureus.

The effects of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP on cell viability and migration in vitro. (a) The in vitro cytotoxicity of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP on HCECs without and with laser treatment (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min). (b, c) The effects of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP on HCECs migration by a scratch assay. (d, e) iNOS protein expression levels in HCECs subjected to different treatments. (f) NO content in HCECs subjected to different treatments. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

Fluorescence imaging diagnosis of bacterial keratitis in vivo. (a) Schematic diagram of the diagnosis and treatment process for bacterial keratitis. (b) Fluorescence images of healthy corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP (named the Normal group) and infected corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA or Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP; the corresponding corneas were examined via slit-lamp microscopy. (c) Corresponding fluorescence intensity of the images in (b). **p < 0.01. (d, e) Fluorescence images of infected corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at different time points. The fluorescence intensity of the treated corneas initially increased, after which the highest fluorescence intensity was reached at 30 min.

Therapeutic efficacy of bacterial keratitis in vivo. (a, b) Photothermal heating images and curves of mouse corneas treated with PBS, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min). (c) Representative slit-lamp images of BALB/c mouse corneas after different treatments. (d) Corresponding clinical score evaluation (0–12), which was quantified by the opacity area, surface regularity, opacity density, and all traces. (e) Fluorescence images of bacterial corneas during the treatments. (f) Corresponding fluorescence intensity of the images in (e). (g, h) Photographs of bacterial colonies on LB agar plates from the corresponding corneas and the number of surviving bacteria in the cornea after different treatments. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.
H&E staining (a) and immunofluorescence staining (b) of BALB/c mouse corneas after different treatments. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.
Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP for the diagnosis and treatment of bacterial keratitis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As bacterial keratitis progresses rapidly, prompt intervention is necessary. Current diagnostic processes are time-consuming and invasive, leading to improper antibiotics for treatment. Therefore, innovative strategies for diagnosing and treating bacterial keratitis are urgently needed. In this study, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were developed by loading nitroreductase-responsive probes (NTRPs) onto Cu2-xSe@BSA. These nanoparticles exhibited integrated fluorescence imaging and antibacterial capabilities. In vitro and in vivo experiments showed that the nanoparticles produced responsive fluorescence signals in bacteria within 30 min due to an interaction between the released NTRP and bacterial endogenous nitroreductase (NTR). When combined with low-temperature photothermal therapy (PTT), the nanoparticles effectively eliminated E. coli and S. aureus, achieved antibacterial efficacy above 95% and facilitated the re-epithelialization process at the corneal wound site in vivo. Overall, the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles demonstrated potential for rapid, noninvasive in situ diagnosis, treatment, and visualization assessment of therapy effectiveness in bacterial keratitis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nitroreductase-responsive nanoparticles for in situ fluorescence imaging and treatment of bacterial keratitis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial keratitis is the most common type of infectious keratitis. As an ocular emergency, the disease progresses rapidly and necessitates urgent intervention [1–3]. The current diagnostic methods for bacterial keratitis, including corneal scraping, bacterial culture, and Gram staining, are traumatic, time-consuming, labor-intensive, and tedious to perform [4,5]. Notably, the percentage of positive culture results is low, ranging from 38% to 66% [6,7]. Clinicians often make empirical diagnoses based on the lesion morphology and clinical manifestations of a patient, then administer tentative treatments with broad-spectrum antibiotics. However, the widespread and indiscriminate use of antibiotics on ocular surfaces leads to increased bacterial resistance [8–10]. Therefore, noninvasive, expeditious, and sensitive in situ diagnostic methods and effective antibacterial strategies for diagnosing and treating bacterial keratitis must be explored to mitigate the issue of drug-resistant bacterial strains.

Fluorescence imaging has emerged as a powerful technique for diagnosing bacterial infections in situ owing to its inherent advantages, such as convenience, high sensitivity, and real-time monitoring capability [11–13]. Various types of fluorescent probes have been developed to improve imaging accuracy [14]. Unlike traditional “always-on” fluorescent probes that always show fluorescence signals under excitation light, activatable fluorescent probes, which consist of fluorophores, linker groups, and recognition groups, are not fluorescent until activated by specific bacterial microenvironments. As a result, these probes demonstrate relatively higher contrast at the targeted site [15,16]. Nitroreductase (NTR) is an endogenous enzyme that is highly expressed in Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [17–19]. Several fluorescent probes responsive to NTR have been reported to exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity and have been successfully used for in vitro bacterial imaging [20,21]. In our previous study, we successfully synthesized a nitroreductase-responsive probe (NTRP) with the chemical formula C43H50IN3O3. This approach achieved the desired turn-on fluorescence imaging in response to NTR [22]. Consequently, the synthesized NTRP shows promise for noninvasive fluorescence imaging-based diagnosis of bacterial keratitis in situ.

In order to mitigate the emergence of antimicrobial resistance and simultaneously enhance antibacterial efficiency, a variety of nanoparticles [23–28], including gold (Au), silver (Ag), Zinc oxide (ZnO), and copper (Cu), have emerged as promising antibacterial agents for combating severe bacterial infections [29–32]. Copper-based nanoparticles are widely used as antibacterial agents for the treatment of bacterial infections due to their exceptional antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [33–35]. Copper ions released from nanoparticles can be cytotoxic and effective at killing bacteria by disrupting cell membranes, denaturing protein structures, or altering enzymatic functions [36]. Additionally, these nanoparticles demonstrate remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency and can rapidly reach effective temperatures for bacterial elimination [37–40]. It is widely acknowledged that bacteria often develop resistance to antibiotics by blocking or reducing drug absorption, enhancing drug metabolism, or promoting drug excretion. In comparison, bacteria that develop resistance to heat pose a greater challenge [41–43]. Therefore, combining copper-based nanoparticles with photothermal therapy (PTT) can effectively eradicate bacteria. Furthermore, copper ions play a significant role in promoting the generation of nitric oxide (NO) from naturally occurring nitrites in wound exudates, as well as in facilitating the expression of iNOS, thus promoting the production of NO [44–47]. This process significantly alleviates keratitis-related inflammation by simultaneously modulating the expression of toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2) and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) [48], thereby facilitating the process of corneal regeneration [49].

The application of multifunctional nanoparticles has emerged as a promising approach for the early diagnosis and therapy of bacterial infections. In the present study, copper-based nanoparticles loaded with NTR-responsive probes were designed and constructed. As depicted in Scheme 1 , bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used as a template for Cu2-xSe@BSA synthesis and as a linker to load the NTRP. Through the electrostatic interaction between negatively charged Cu2-xSe@BSA and positively charged NTRP, the NTRP was successfully loaded onto Cu2-xSe@BSA, resulting in the formation of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles. We systematically investigated the NTR- and bacteria-responsive fluorescence imaging capabilities, antibacterial performance, and potential for promoting corneal healing of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles in vitro and in vivo. Notably, these nanoparticles exhibited pH-dependent NTRP release behavior and bacteria-responsive fluorescence imaging capability. After the nanoparticles were topically administered to infected corneas in mouse models, increased NTRP release was achieved in a weakly acidic environment; as a result, bacteria-responsive fluorescence imaging was facilitated through an interaction between the released NTRP and the bacterial endogenous NTR. Furthermore, the nanoparticles effectively eliminated bacteria through the synergistic effects of copper ions in combination with low-temperature PTT. Moreover, the presence of copper ions further accelerated re-epithelialization and wound healing in the corneas. Consequently, the application of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles offers a promising platform for highly responsive imaging diagnosis, efficient treatment and visualization assessment of therapy effectiveness in bacterial keratitis, serving as a supplementary diagnostic and therapeutic tool for this condition.

Sodium selenite (Na2SeO3) and copper chloride (CuCl2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), ascorbic acid, dichloromethane (DCM), dimethylformamide (DMF), phosphorus oxychloride, and acetic anhydride (Ac2O) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and E. coli (ATCC 25922) were obtained from Bena Culture Collection (Henan, China), human corneal epithelial cells (HCECs) were obtained from Sun Yat-sen University (Guangzhou, China), and BALB/c mice were obtained from Nanjing Cavens Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China).

NTRP was synthesized according to a protocol described in our previous work [22]. Briefly, 10 mL of DCM, 10 mL of DMF, 9.25 mL of phosphorus oxychloride, and 2.6 mL of cyclohexanone were mixed and refluxed at 80 °C under a N2 atmosphere for 3 h to generate compound 1. Then, 4.87 g of 2,3,3-trimethylindolenine and 10.2 g of 1-iodopropane were dissolved in 20 mL of acetonitrile and stirred at 78 °C for 28 h to generate compound 2. Next, 0.68 g of compound 1 and 2.62 g of compound 2 were dissolved in 20 mL of Ac2O. The mixture was stirred at 80 °C for 40 min to form compound 3. Compound 3 was then dissolved in DMF with sodium acetate anhydrous (AcONa) and stirred at 80 °C for 5 h to generate compound 4, known as 2,6-bis(2-(3,3-dimethyl-1-propylindolin-2-ylidene) ethylidene)-cyclohexanone (HQO). Finally, 0.45 g of the fluorophore HQO, 0.42 g of 4-nitrobenzyl bromide and 0.60 g of sodium iodide were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF and stirred at room temperature overnight. Subsequently, the mixture was washed with water and purified by silica gel column chromatography to generate the final product, NTRP, which was further dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and stored at −20 °C for further use.

Cu2-xSe@BSA was prepared according to previous methods [50–52]. In brief, 50 mg of BSA was dissolved in Milli-Q water and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of 2% Na2SeO3 and 2.5 mL of 10% ascorbic acid were added to the solution, and the mixture was allowed to react until it turned orange‒red. Moreover, 5 mL of 1.5% CuCl2 and 3 mL of 10% ascorbic acid were reacted at room temperature until the mixture became a white suspension. Finally, the white suspension was added to the orange‒red mixture under continuous stirring. After reacting for 30 h, dark green products were generated. The resultant products were centrifuged and washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to generate the Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles.

Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was prepared in a dark environment as follows: 1.84 mL of Cu2-xSe@BSA (174 μg/mL) was mixed with 0.16 mL of NTRP (1 mM) and stirred for 1 h. After centrifugation was performed and the mixture was washed with water until NTRP was undetectable in the supernatant, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was obtained and dispersed in PBS for further use.

The loading rate and loading content of the NTRP on the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were calculated using the following equation:
(1) Loading rate of NTRP (%) = the weight of NTRP loaded on the nanoparticles / the total weight of added NTRP
(2) Loading content (μmol NTRP / mg Cu2-xSe@BSA) = the weight of NTRP loaded on the nanoparticles / (MNTRP × the weight of the Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles)

The morphology and elemental distribution of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP were characterized using a 120 kV transmission electron microscope (Bio-TEM, Hitachi HT7800, Japan) and a 200 kV transmission electron microscope (TEM, Thermo Fisher Talos F200X, FEI, USA). The loading capacity of the NTRP on Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was determined via ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis, Lambda 750, PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to characterize the elemental and chemical state information of the nanoparticles. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the nanoparticles were measured using a particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance and ion release behaviors of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate their photothermal properties, the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were dispersed in Milli-Q water and subjected to 808 nm NIR laser irradiation at room temperature. The real-time temperature was monitored using an infrared thermal imager (Optris PI450i, Germany). To test the impact of laser power density on photothermal performance, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP dispersions at different power densities (0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, and 1.5 W/cm2) were irradiated with an 808 nm NIR laser at a concentration of 40 μg/mL. Additionally, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP dispersions at different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 80, and 160 μg/mL) were exposed to the 808 nm NIR laser at a power density of 1 W/cm2 for 10 min. Milli-Q water was used as a control.

Photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) was calculated as follows [53]:

To evaluate the effect of photothermal performance on the release of copper ions, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at a concentration of 80 μg/mL in PBS was irradiated with the 808 nm NIR laser (1 W/cm2). After irradiation for different durations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 60 min), the nanoparticles were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and the concentration of copper ions in the supernatant was measured using inductively coupled plasma‒optical emission spectrometry (ICP‒OES; SPECTRO, Germany). Furthermore, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was dispersed in PBS at different pH values (5.5, 6.5, and 7.4) to evaluate its copper ion release behavior after incubation for 30 min, 1 h, and 2 h. The nanoparticles were then centrifuged, and the copper ion concentration was measured as described above.

The Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles, with concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/mL, were separately mixed with the bacterial suspensions. These mixtures were then exposed with and without an 808 nm laser at a power density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min. After an additional 24 h of incubation, the bacterial suspensions were transferred to 96-well plates, and their concentrations were assessed by measuring the optical density at 600 nm using a microplate reader (SpectraMax 190, Molecular Devices, USA). Additionally, 50 μL of each diluted suspension was evenly distributed on LB plates and incubated at 37 °C for an additional 24 h.

The LIVE/DEAD BacLight kit (Thermo Fisher L7012, USA) was used to assess bacterial viability following different treatments. Briefly, equal volumes of SYTO 9 and PI were mixed, and 3 μL of the mixture was added to the bacterial suspension. After incubating in the dark for 15 min, the bacteria were observed using a laser scanning confocal microscope (TCS, SP8, Leica, Germany). For this assessment, the excitation wavelength for SYTO9 (green, live bacteria) was 488 nm, and the emission wavelength was 518–540 nm. The excitation wavelength for PI (red, dead bacteria) was 552 nm, and the emission wavelength was 580–617 nm. Furthermore, the structural morphology of the bacteria was evaluated. The bacteria were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and sequentially treated with ethanol–water mixtures (30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, 95%, 100%) at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, the dried bacteria were sputter-coated with gold and observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8230, Japan) for morphological analysis. Additionally, the treated bacteria were fixed with glutaraldehyde and osmic acid, dehydrated with a series of ethanol–water mixtures, embedded, and finally sliced for TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell cytotoxicity and migration
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytotoxicity of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles was assessed using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8; Dojindo, Kyushu Island, Japan). HCECs were seeded into 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were exposed to different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60, and 80 μg/mL) of the nanoparticle suspensions and irradiated with an 808 nm laser. The power density of the laser was set at 0.5 W/cm2, and the irradiation time was 10 min. The cells were further incubated for 24 h. Afterward, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for another 45 min. Finally, the absorbance of the cell suspensions was measured at 450 nm. In the cell migration assay, the cells were starved in FBS-free medium for 24 h and then subjected to a scratch assay using a 200 μL pipette tip to create a straight scratch. After two washes with PBS were performed, the cells were treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at a concentration of 80 μg/mL with or without laser irradiation (10 min, 0.5 W/cm2). Subsequently, the cells were incubated in media containing 1% FBS at 37 °C for 24 h, after which the cell migration rate was assessed by imaging and analysis.

All protocols used for the animal experiments were approved by the Animal Welfare and Ethical Committee of Tongji University (SHDSYY-2023). To establish animal models of bacterial keratitis, BALB/c mice were anesthetized, and their corneal epithelium was gently scraped under a stereomicroscope. Subsequently, 10 μL of S. aureus suspension at a concentration of 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL was instilled into the corneal wound and left undisturbed for 10 min, followed by an incubation of 24 h. The animal models of bacterial keratitis were considered established when corneal epithelial defects, indistinct margins of infiltration, and corneal stromal edema were observed under a slit-lamp microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging diagnosis and therapy for bacterial keratitis
ce:section.ce:para: For imaging-based diagnosis of bacterial keratitis in vivo, 10 μL of solution containing 80 μg/mL Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP (with an NTRP concentration of 10.8 μM) and Cu2-xSe@BSA were topically administered onto the surface of mice cornea. After the nanoparticles remained on the corneal surface for different durations, the cornea was moistened with PBS, and fluorescence images were captured using a fluorescence stereoscopic microscope (SAX12, SUNNY, China).

The mice with bacterial keratitis were divided into the following groups: a PBS group, a Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP group, and a Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group. Specifically, 10 μL of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP (with NTRP concentration at 10.8 μM) and Cu2-xSe@BSA solutions at a concentration of 80 μg/mL were added to the cornea. In the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group, the mice were irradiated with an 808 nm laser for 10 min, during which the temperature around the cornea was measured and recorded. The treatments lasted for 5 days in total. To evaluate therapeutic efficacy, slit-lamp microscopy images of all eyes were captured before infection, before treatment (day 0), and at 1, 3, and 5 days after treatment. Three clinicians evaluated the cornea based on the following factors [54,55]: corneal surface morphology, opacity area, and density. Each factor was scored numerically from 0 to 4, in which 4 denoted the most severe condition and 0 denoted the least severe condition of the cornea. The cumulative score of each parameter was subsequently used to determine the overall biomicroscopic examination score for each cornea.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP in vivo, mice were treated with PBS, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, or Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser. After continuous treatment for 5 days, the body weights of the mice were recorded every two days over a duration of 21 days. Finally, routine blood indices and blood biochemical indices were measured, and the organs were observed following H&E staining.

Each experiment was repeated in triplicate. All the data processing and statistical analyses were carried out with SPSS 22.0. The single factor analysis of variance method was used for statistical analysis, with p values < 0.05/0.01 indicating statistical significance. The graphs were generated with OriginPro 2022.

Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was prepared by loading NTRP onto Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles. First, Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles were obtained using a facile oxidation‒reduction method as described in previous studies [50–52]. In this method, selenium nanoparticles, which were obtained by reducing Na2SeO3 with ascorbic acid, served as sacrificial templates and reacted with Cu+ to produce copper selenide nanoparticles. The nanoparticles were gradually oxidized upon exposure to air, resulting in the formation of Cu2-xSe@BSA. The Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles exhibited a spherical shape with an average particle size of approximately 30 nm (Fig. 1 a) and clear lattice fringes with a distance of 0.203 nm (Figure S1a). In addition, the copper and selenium were distributed uniformly within the structure (Figure S1b). The XRD pattern confirmed the presence of characteristic diffraction peaks consistent with cubic berzelianite (Cu2-xSe, JCPDS NO. 06–0680) (Figure S2a). Furthermore, the XPS spectra of Cu 2p confirmed that Cu+ and Cu2+ coexisted in the nanoparticles, and Cu+ was the predominant component at approximately 80.12% (Figure S2b-c). Overall, these results demonstrated that the Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles were successfully synthesized.

The NTR-responsive probe (NTRP) was synthesized following our previous study [22], and its chemical structure and mass spectrum are presented in Figure S3. Given that Cu2-xSe@BSA and NTRP are negatively and positively charged, respectively, it is reasonable that NTRP can be successfully loaded onto Cu2-xSe@BSA through electrostatic adsorption. As shown in Fig. 1b–d, the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were spherical, and their particle size (Fig. 1b), lattice structure (Fig. 1c), and element distribution (Fig. 1d) closely resembled those of the Cu2-xSe@BSA nanoparticles. Therefore, the incorporation of NTRP did not compromise the structural characteristics of Cu2-xSe@BSA. DLS measurements revealed that the average hydrodynamic particle sizes of Cu2-xSe@BSA and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP were 64.21 nm and 89.19 nm, respectively (Fig. 1e), and their corresponding zeta potentials were −20.96 ± 0.98 mV and −9.01 ± 0.70 mV, respectively (Fig. 1f). These results confirmed that the NTRP was successfully loaded onto Cu2-xSe@BSA, leading to the formation of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles.

The responsiveness of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP to NTR was characterized using UV–vis spectroscopy and a fluorescence imaging system. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 a reveal that Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited an absorption peak at 780 nm, corresponding to the characteristic peak of the NTRP. This observation provided additional evidence for the successful synthesis of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP. Additionally, the loading rate and loading content of NTRP on per milligram of Cu2-xSe@BSA were determined to be 27%, and 135 μmol NTRP/mg Cu2-xSe@BSA using the standard curve in Figure S4b. According to our previous study, NTRP is a small-molecule fluorescent probe that exhibits responsive fluorescence to NTR, upon interaction with NTR, the aromatic nitro group on NTRP was reduced to amino group. This reduction then initiated a 1,6-rearrangement-elimination reaction, ultimately resulting in the generation of the fluorophore HQO (Figure S5) [22]. Furthermore, the color of NTRP changed from green to orange, the absorption intensity at approximately 780 nm decreased and a new absorption peak appeared at approximately 520 nm (Figure S6). Similarly, after Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP reacted with NTR, the absorption intensity significantly decreased at 780 nm and increased at approximately 400–550 nm (Fig. 2b). Under the excitation light with a wavelength of 520 nm, the mixture showed an emission peak at 620 nm (Fig. 2c). Additionally, the mass spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum (Figure S7) of the reaction supernatant confirmed that the fluorescent group HQO formed, indicating that the nanoparticles exhibited NTR responsiveness. To demonstrate the detection limit of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP for NTR, varying concentrations of NTR were mixed with the nanoparticles. The results clearly indicate a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity with rising NTR concentrations, leading to the derivation of a linear equation “Fluorescence Intensity = 31.86 × C (ng/mL) + 6382.71 (R2 = 0.996)”. Through this equation, the detection limit was calculated to be 21.19 ng/mL (Figure S8).

Interestingly, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP also exhibited pH-dependent release of NTRP. Specifically, after the nanoparticles were treated with PBS at pH 6.5 and 5.5, the absorption spectrum of the supernatant at 780 nm increased (Figure S9), indicating that more NTRP was released in acidic environments. Following NTR treatment, the absorbance intensity at 780 nm gradually decreased as the pH of the PBS decreased, whereas the absorbance intensity within 400–550 nm gradually increased (Fig. 2d). Fig. 2e and f further confirmed the close relationship between the pH and responsive fluorescence imaging performance of the nanoparticles, as the fluorescence intensity of the reaction system was highest when the pH of PBS was 5.5 and decreased as the pH increased. These phenomena confirmed our hypothesis that the release of NTRP from the nanoparticles was influenced by the pH of the reaction system, which can be attributed to the isoelectric point of BSA (4.7). As the pH of the solution decreased from 7.4 to 5.5, the electrostatic attraction between BSA and the NTRP gradually decreased. Consequently, the release of NTRP increased when Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was exposed to a solution with a lower pH. Therefore, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP can improve NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging in acidic environments, potentially improving the nanoparticle's ability to image bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance and copper ion release
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have indicated that copper-based nanoparticles can convert near-infrared light into heat with a high efficacy due to localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [51,56,57]. As depicted in Fig. 3 , Figure S10 and Figure S11, the photothermal performance of the nanoparticles was influenced by the nanoparticle concentration and power density of the near-infrared light. With increasing nanoparticle concentration, the temperature of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP mixture gradually increased (Fig. 3a, Figure S10). At a concentration of 160 μg/mL, the temperature increased from 25.5 °C to 73.6 °C within 10 min. In contrast, the temperature of Milli-Q water increased to 24.8 °C after irradiation for 10 min. In addition, the temperature of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP increased with increasing irradiation power density (Fig. 3b). When the concentration of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was 40 μg/mL, the temperature reached 56 °C at an irradiation power density of 1 W/cm2. To evaluate the durability of the photothermal performance, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was subjected to a series of laser on/off cycles for a total of five repetitions. The resulting heating and cooling curves shown in Fig. 3c consistently displayed a recurring pattern, indicating the excellent photothermal conversion stability of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles, achieving a photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of up to 48.9% (Fig. 3d). Therefore, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited favorable photothermal performance upon exposure to 808 nm near-infrared light.

The release of copper ions is primarily influenced by temperature and environmental pH. Laser irradiation of nanoparticles directly induces an increase in temperature, thereby stimulating the release of copper ions [58]. To confirm this, the amounts of copper ions released from Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP without and with laser irradiation were measured. After 1 h of laser irradiation, approximately 1.75% of the copper ions were released into the solution, while 0.43% of the copper ions were released in the group without laser irradiation (Fig. 3e). To investigate the influence of pH on the release of copper ions, the nanoparticles were dispersed in PBS at pH 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the highest amount of copper ions (0.825%) was released in PBS at a pH of 5.5. These findings provide evidence that photothermal effects and weakly acidic conditions increase the release of copper ions from Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, which might be beneficial for synergistically improving antibacterial efficiency through the combination of low-temperature PTT.

Nitroreductase (NTR) is an intracellular enzyme that is highly expressed in gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria and can selectively identify and degrade aromatic compounds that contain nitro groups [17]. Due to the characteristic, NTR substrates is important for bacterial detection and identification [59]. The Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP constructed in this study was demonstrated to exhibit desirable NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging properties. To assess the responsiveness of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP for bacterial fluorescence imaging, E. coli and S. aureus were selected as representative models of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 , before the nanoparticles and bacteria interacted, neither the suspensions of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nor the bacteria showed any fluorescence. In contrast, a discernible fluorescent signal was observed after the bacteria were exposed to Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP for different durations. As the reaction time was extended, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, reaching its greatest value at 6 h. Additionally, according to the confocal images (Figure S12), distinct red signals were observed in both Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP-treated E. coli and S. aureus, indicating the in situ generation of fluorescence signals after the incubation. As time increased to 24 h, the fluorescence intensity showed a decreasing trend. Based on these observations, the synthetic organic probe NTRP, which is released by Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, underwent a reaction with NTR secreted by bacteria and led to the production of the fluorescent compound HQO. As time extended to 6 h, the increase in HQO concentration led to enhanced fluorescence intensity. However, with further extension of time, NTRP might be exhausted and the generated HQO might degrade in the bacteria solution, resulting in a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. In general, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP effectively exhibited a responsive fluorescence capability of imaging bacteria by interacting with NTR secreted from living bacteria.

E. coli and S. aureus were used to evaluate the antibacterial properties of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, both with and without laser irradiation. Under laser irradiation (0.5 W/cm2, 10 min), the temperature of the bacterial suspension gradually increased with increasing concentrations of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, reaching approximately 47 °C at a concentration of 80 μg/mL (Fig. 5 a). After incubation with different concentrations of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, the optical density of the bacteria at 600 nm was measured. As shown in Fig. 5b and c, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited low antibacterial effects in the absence of laser irradiation due to the antibacterial properties of copper ions [60]. Specifically, when the nanoparticle concentration was 80 μg/mL, the inhibitory effects against E. coli and S. aureus were 35.67% and 37.62%, respectively. In contrast, when exposed to laser irradiation, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited significant antibacterial efficacy against both strains of bacteria, with antibacterial efficacy exceeding 95%. Additionally, the colony forming unit (CFU) assay confirmed the antibacterial efficacy of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser (Figure S13 and Fig. 5d and e). With the increase in concentration of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, the bacterial survival rate decreased sequentially. However, even when the concentration reached 120 μg/mL, the survival rates of E. coli and S. aureus remained at 48.69% and 48.98%, respectively. In contrast, after irradiation with an 808 nm laser, the survival rates of E. coli treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at concentrations of 20, 40, 80, and 120 μg/mL were 70%, 55%, 6% and 2%, respectively. Similarly, the survival rates of S. aureus treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP at the above concentrations were 76%, 58%, 8% and 1%. These results can be attributed to the synergistic antibacterial effect provided by copper ions and photothermal effects. Moreover, consistent with previous research findings [32,58], the increase in temperature and the bacterial microenvironment effectively facilitated the release of antibacterial copper ions, further increasing the synergistic antibacterial effects of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP.

The LIVE/DEAD BacLight Kit was used to assess the influence of Cu2-xSe@BSA on the viability of E. coli and S. aureus. Consistent with the above results, few bacteria in the control group exhibited susceptibility to PI staining, as indicated by the red signal in the fluorescence images of E. coli (Fig. 6 a) and S. aureus (Fig. 6b). In contrast, approximately half of the bacteria in the Cu2-xSe@BSA group were stained red, while nearly all bacteria in the Cu2-xSe@BSA + laser group were stained red. The depth of PI penetration into bacteria depends on the integrity of the cell membrane. The results suggest that in the Cu2-xSe@BSA + laser group, the integrity of the cell wall or membrane was disrupted in most bacteria, indicating the significant bactericidal effect of the laser-irradiated Cu2-xSe@BSA and providing additional evidence for synergistic therapy with copper ions and PTT.

To further confirm this inference, the morphological changes in the bacteria were analyzed by SEM and TEM. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, both strains of bacteria in the control group maintained their integrity, roundness, smooth surface, and shape. However, after irradiation with an 808 nm laser, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and the resultant heat severely damaged the structure of E. coli and S. aureus by distorting their morphology; in addition, clear lesions and holes were generated in their cell wall/membranes. The TEM images provided additional evidence for the disruptive effect of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP on bacterial structures. As can be seen in Fig. 7b, the cell wall and membrane structure of bacteria were effectively disrupted by Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser. Additionally, some nanoparticles could be observed within the damaged bacteria (Figure S14). The disruption of the bacterial structure led to the leakage of cellular contents, eventually resulting in bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP on cell viability and migration in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP to HCECs was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. When subjected to laser irradiation at a power density of 0.5 W/cm2 for 10 min, the temperature of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP increased to approximately 45 °C, as depicted in Figure S15. The viability of the HCECs incubated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP remained above 92.7% without laser treatment and above 87.5% with laser treatment (Fig. 8 a). These results indicate the low cytotoxicity of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP in vitro. Previous studies have demonstrated that copper ions can promote the regeneration of corneal epithelium [46,58]. Here, an in vitro scratch assay was employed to confirm the effect of copper ions on the regeneration of corneal epithelial tissue. As depicted in Fig. 8b, HCECs in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group exhibited the highest migration capability, followed by those in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP group. In accordance with the optical observations, quantitative analysis (Fig. 8c) revealed that the cells in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group exhibited the highest cell migration rate (81.6%), followed by those in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP group (55.9%), and those in the control group (23.5%). The expression of iNOS serves as an indicator of wound healing initiation due to its ability to promote the generation of nitric oxide (NO), which subsequently plays a role in promoting epithelial cell migration and wound re-epithelialization [44–47]. Copper ions have been shown to stimulate the expression of iNOS [61], suggesting that copper ions released from Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP can enhance cell migration through upregulating iNOS expression. As shown in Fig. 8d and e, the expression of iNOS in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group was approximately 65% greater than that in the control group. In addition, the NO content in HCECs after different treatments was also examined. As shown in Fig. 8f, the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group had the highest concentration of NO (3.38 μM), which is 2.2 times that of the control group. This concentration effectively promotes the normal turnover and vitality of ocular surface cells, as well as the migration of HCECs. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that this concentration is significantly lower than the concentration of 30 μM that induces damage to HCECs [46,62,63].Therefore, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibited good in vitro biocompatibility and the capacity to promote the migration of HCECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo diagnosis and therapy of bacterial keratitis
ce:section.ce:para: The Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles were used to diagnose and treat bacterial keratitis in vivo. As shown in Fig. 9 a, S. aureus was administered to the corneas of BALB/c mice, leading to the establishment of animal models of bacterial keratitis after 24 h. Subsequently, Cu2-xSe@BSA and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP suspensions were added to the corneas, and fluorescence images and slit-lamp images were captured after 30 min. The infected corneas that were treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP showed a clear fluorescence signal, as depicted in Fig. 9b. Conversely, the healthy corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP (Normal group) and the infected corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA did not display an observable fluorescence signal. The quantitative analysis shown in Fig. 9c further indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP-treated infected corneas was approximately 20-fold greater than that of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP-treated normal group. These findings suggest that Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP exhibits a notable capability of performing bacteria-responsive fluorescence imaging and shows potential as a “turn-on” fluorescent agent for the diagnosis of bacterial keratitis. Furthermore, in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP-treated bacterial keratitis group, fluorescence images were taken at different time points to determine the optimal time for in vivo diagnosis. Fig. 9d and e illustrate that the treated cornea showed a noticeable fluorescence signal as early as 10 min after treatment, with the fluorescence intensity gradually increasing and reaching a maximum at 30 min. As time progressed, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased due to tear washing. In general, after the application of the nanoparticle onto the cornea, a noticeable fluorescence signal could be observed within the time window of 20 min to 2 h. It is believed that the nanoparticle provided an imaging time window ranged from 20 min to 2 h for the effective monitoring of infected corneas. These findings suggest that Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP can rapidly detect bacterial keratitis in a noninvasive manner, providing an alternative or auxiliary approach for in situ diagnosis of infections.

To minimize the undesirable damage caused by excessive heat to surrounding healthy tissues, an alternative hyperthermia treatment known as low-temperature PTT has been implemented. This treatment typically involves maintaining the temperature below 48 °C [64,65] and is considered as a promising therapeutic approach [66–68]. In addition, the optimum temperature for heat therapy for the eye is approximately 40–45 °C [69]. To evaluate the synergistic efficacy of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP with low-temperature PTT for the treatment of bacterial keratitis caused by S. aureus, infected corneas were subjected to different treatments (PBS, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser) for 5 days (1 drop/time, 1 time/day). Slit-lamp images, fluorescence imaging, and corneal clinical scores were obtained and recorded on days 0, 1, 3, and 5 (Fig. 9a).

In the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group, the temperature in the specific region of the corneal wound gradually increased and eventually reached 43 °C after irradiation for 10 min (Fig. 10 a and b). Additionally, the real-time thermal imaging demonstrated that the nanoparticles remained on the cornea for more than 10 min, providing enough time for low-temperature PTT of infected corneas. In contrast, the temperature of the corneal wound in the PBS group remained at 32 °C, which may indicate a lack of effective antibacterial activity. As shown in Fig. 10c, the healthy corneas were initially clear and transparent; however, after a 24-h infection period, severe symptoms such as corneal opacity and edema were apparent, and the iris texture and pupil contour were indiscernible. These symptoms were observed across all corneas; high clinical scores (∼10) with no significant differences were initially attained for the mice. Therefore, a similar degree of bacterial infection occurred in all groups at the beginning of treatment. After 5 days of treatment, symptoms associated with bacterial keratitis in the corneas were not significantly relieved in the control group, and the corneas became more turbid and edematous, with a score increasing to 12. In addition, the corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP were misty, swollen, and cloudy, with a score of 5, although some improvements in corneal opacity and edema were observed. In contrast, corneas treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser were transparent, with the lowest score being 2 (Fig. 10c and d).

To verify the potential of the nanoparticles for diagnosis-lead therapy, we performed fluorescence imaging of the corneas of mice before the treatment of bacterial keratitis. As shown in Fig. 10e and f, after different treatments for bacterial keratitis, the fluorescence signals at the cornea gradually decreased. Especially in the group treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser, the fluorescent intensity was the lowest on day 5. These results are consistent with the preliminary observations made through slit lamp examination. In addition, the corneas of mice treated for 0, 1, 3, and 5 days were removed, and their NTR concentrations were measured. Following treatment with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser for 5 days, the NTR concentration decreased from 72.24 μg/mL to 2.34 μg/mL (Figure S16), significantly lower than the NTR content (47.23 μg/mL) in the cornea treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP alone. Based on the gradually decreasing fluorescence intensity of corneas, the application of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP offers an approximate evaluation of the NTR content in the corneas. To further determine the antibacterial efficacy of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser, the number of residual bacteria on the corneas was measured. As depicted in Fig. 10g and h, fewer bacterial colonies were observed in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP group than in the control group. Notably, no bacterial colonies were observed in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group. Accordingly, favorable antibacterial properties were attained using Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP in combination with laser irradiation to treat bacterial keratitis. These findings indicate that nanoparticles have the potential to be utilized in the diagnosis and treatment of bacterial keratitis, as well as in the assessment of therapy effectiveness.

After 5 days of therapy, the cornea in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group appeared transparent and showed no signs of swelling; in addition, the outer layer of the cornea remained undamaged, as indicated by the results of corneal sodium fluorescein staining (Figure S17). Furthermore, based on the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of corneal tissues shown in Fig. 11 a, the corneal structure in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group remained intact and resembled that of a healthy cornea. Previous studies have highlighted the significant role of copper ions in corneal wound healing by stimulating the production of NO. NO not only promotes the migration of HCECs but also potentially contributes to corneal healing by inhibiting inflammatory damage [46,48,68]. After Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP was topically administered to infected corneas, the copper ions released in the Cu+ state interacted with naturally occurring nitrites in the wound exudate to generate NO, which promoted the migration of epithelial cells as well as the synthesis and deposition of collagen [46]. Copper ions also induce the expression of iNOS, further increasing the total amount of NO in cells and ultimately facilitating the healing of corneal wounds [44]. Additionally, the release of NO can downregulate TNF-α expression, which can induce NF-κB depression and exert a potent inhibitory effect on inflammatory damage to the cornea [48]. The expression levels of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α were validated in vivo using corneal immunofluorescence, as shown in Fig. 11b. Compared with those in the other groups, the expression levels of these inflammatory factors were lower in the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser group. These findings demonstrated that the application of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser is effective for the treatment of bacterial keratitis in vivo via synergistic therapy involving low-temperature PTT and copper ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary evaluation of in vivo biocompatibility
ce:section.ce:para: The body weight of mice during treatment is an essential factor during biocompatibility evaluations, as the weight is used for early assessments of the general health of animals [70,71]. Following treatment with PBS, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP or Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser for 5 days, the body weight of the mice in all groups gradually increased over a period of 21 days, as depicted in Figure S18a. This observation suggests that the topical administration of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP to corneas, with and without laser irradiation, did not result in significant detrimental effects. Moreover, common blood test results and blood biochemical indices, including white blood cell (WBC) count, red blood cell (RBC) count, hemoglobin (HGB) level, mean corpuscular volume (MCV), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), and platelet count (PLT), were within reasonable ranges compared to those of the control group and the reference values (Figure S18b-g). Furthermore, the alanine transaminase (ALT) and aspartate transaminase (AST) levels, which indicate liver function, as well as the creatinine (CREA) and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) levels, which indicate kidney function, were within the established normal ranges (Figure S18h-k). Moreover, H&E staining of the corneas, eyeballs, retinas, and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed in the PBS, Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP and Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser groups, and no discernible pathological changes were observed (Figure S19, 20). These findings strongly suggest that the application of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP + laser does not cause any significant adverse effects or organ toxicity.

In summary, NTR-responsive copper-based nanoparticles, denoted as Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP, were successfully developed by loading NTRP onto Cu2-xSe@BSA through electrostatic interactions. These nanoparticles exhibited several desirable characteristics, including NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging capability, antibacterial properties, and satisfactory biocompatibility. Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP demonstrated greater NTRP release in an acidic environment, which was beneficial for facilitating NTR-responsive fluorescence imaging when interacting with bacteria in vitro. Moreover, the nanoparticles gradually released copper ions and generated heat upon exposure to 808 nm laser irradiation. Through these characteristics, the nanoparticles exhibited synergistic antibacterial effects in vitro and in vivo. Notably, upon the administration of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP to infected corneas, distinct fluorescence signals appeared within 10 min, peaking at 30 min. The infected corneas could be completely cured within 5 days through the combination of low-temperature photothermal therapy (<43 °C) and copper ions therapy. Moreover, these nanoparticles did not exhibit apparent cytotoxicity during treatment. Therefore, the developed responsive fluorescent imaging and antibacterial nanoparticles show great potential for noninvasive in situ diagnosis, treatment, and visualization assessment of therapy effectiveness in bacterial keratitis.

Supporting Information: Materials; TEM characterizations of Cu2-xSe@BSA; XRD and XPS characterizations of Cu2-xSe@BSA; Chemical structure and mass spectrum of the NTRP; UV–Vis Spectra of NTRP; Chemical structure of HQO; The photothermal performance of Cu2-xSe@BSA; Confocal fluorescence images of bacteria treated with Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP; CFU assay of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP without laser irradiation; High-resolution images and EDS mapping; Photothermal performance of Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP incubated with cells; NTR concentration in corneas and images of corneal fluorescein sodium staining; Preliminary toxicity assay of the Cu2-xSe@BSA@NTRP nanoparticles; and H&E staining of the major organs and corneas of BALB/c mice with different treatments on day 21 after treatment.
